title: 2.5 Seeds
description: Seeds — every fact from the textbook.

questions.question: What are the three parts of a seed?
options: Seed embryo, cotyledon/endosperm and seed coat
questions.correctAnswer: Seed embryo, cotyledon/endosperm and seed coat

questions.question: What is the seed additionally covered by in angiosperms?
Why are angiosperm seeds called covered seeds?
options: They have a thick seed coat
They are additionally covered by the fruit
questions.correctAnswer: They are additionally covered by the fruit

questions.question: What are gymnosperm seeds called because they have nothing except their own seed coat?
What does the seed embryo consist of?
What does the radicle develop into?
What does the plumule develop into?
What is the function of cotyledon and endosperm?
options: They are food storing tissues essential for the seed embryo until it forms leaf and starts manufacturing its own food
questions.correctAnswer: They are food storing tissues essential for the seed embryo until it forms leaf and starts manufacturing its own food

questions.question: How many cotyledons does a dicot seed have?
How many cotyledons does a monocot seed have?
How are dicot cotyledons described in terms of food storage?
How are monocot cotyledons described in terms of food storage?
options: Very thin and lack food materials
They store large amounts of food
questions.correctAnswer: Very thin and lack food materials

questions.question: What is the status of endosperm in dicot seeds?
What is the status of endosperm in monocot seeds?
What happens to the primary root in monocot seeds?
options: It is replaced by adventitious fibrous roots
It grows deep into the soil
questions.correctAnswer: It is replaced by adventitious fibrous roots

questions.question: What does the primary root produced from the radicle in dicot seeds do?
options: It is replaced by adventitious roots
It becomes a fibrous root system
